data.title: The War Escalates And Markets Are Unimpressed With IEA Oil Move
data.description:
The Middle East war is escalating. The market seems unimpressed with the announcement that strategic oil reserves will be released. The announcement is light on details, such as pace and composition. Moreover, the markets’ understanding of the disruption is well beyond oil and includes gas, fertilizer, sulfur, and urea. Adding to geoeconomic morass, late yesterday, the US announced it was opening trade investigations (under Section 301 of the Trade Act) to around 16 countries, including China, the EU, Mexico, Japan, South Korea, Taiwan, Switzerland, Norway, and several others in East Asia.

The US dollar (DXY) is mostly firmer. Equities are lower and rates are mostly higher. April WTI is above yesterday’s high, as is May Brent. Gold and silver are consolidating but slightly firmer.

Prices

G10

• The euro trended lower in Europe and North America yesterday. It reached nearly $1.1560, and selling earlier today took it to almost $1.1530. The four-month low set on Monday was slightly above $1.1505. It recovered back to yesterday’s low in Europe but stalled. The technical tone is poor, and although the momentum indicators are stretched, they do not appear poised to turn higher.

• The dollar stalled slightly in front of JPY159 in North America yesterday and edged up to almost JPY159.25 today. It held the January 23 high (~JPY159.25) when the Fed reportedly checked prices on behalf of the US Treasury. The dollar slipped to session lows a little ahead of JPY158.65 in Europe, where it appears to be finding bids.

• Sterling slipped below $1.34 in the North American session yesterday, but it did not spur fresh selling. It spent most of the NY afternoon consolidating in a narrow range below $1.3415, before being sold to about $1.3360 in Asia-Pacific turnover today. It has returned to the $1.3400 area in Europe but may be running out of steam. Options for almost GBP500 at $1.3375 expire today. Sterling has outperformed the euro since the war began and policy expectations have shifted. The euro traded at a two-month high near GBP0.8800 at the end of February and fell to almost GBP0.8620 yesterday, which has held so far today. A six-month low was recorded in early February slightly below GBP0.8615

• The US dollar poked above CAD1.36 briefly yesterday to take out Tuesday’s high, but the consolidative tone remains intact. It flirted with the CAD1.36 level today, but after slipping to almost CAD1.3575, it found new bids. The greenback remained below the week’s high set Monday, slightly below CAD1.3610, where options for almost $400 mln expire today. A move above CAD1.3615 would spur near-term gains toward CAD1.3640-50.

• After reaching almost $0.7190, a marginal new session high in North America and its best level since mid-2022, the Australian dollar consolidated around the middle of the day’s range. It stalled today and is consolidating between about $0.7110 and $0.7160.

EM

• The risk-off mood dampened the enthusiasm for the Mexican peso yesterday and most emerging market currencies. The peso surrendered about half of the gains recorded in the previous two sessions. The dollar extended its gains today and entered a resistance band in the MXN17.75-80 area before being sold in Europe back toward session lows (~MXN17.64). Options about $400 mln at MXN17.61 expire today.

• The dollar consolidated mostly between CNH6.86 and CNH6.88 yesterday. It reached CNH6.8855 today before coming off. The session low (~CNH6.8675) was recorded in the European morning. The PBOC set the dollar’s fix at CNY6.8959 (CNY6.8917 yesterday). Beijing does not appear to be taking advantage of the current market volatility to weaken the yuan or harass Taiwan.

• The dollar reached marginal record high today against the Indian rupee, near INR92.3640. There have been some reports of central bank intervention. Dollar sales drain rupee liquidity from the banking system, and the central bank conducted foreign exchange swaps to counter the drain.

Other Markets

• Equities are under pressure. The MSCI Asia Pacific Index fell for the first time in three sessions today. Europe’s Stoxx 600 (STOXX) is off about 0.25%, and US index futures (SPX) are 0.25-0.40% lower.

• The US 10-year yield (US10Y) rose 13 bp in the past two sessions, including slightly more than seven basis points yesterday. Although the 10-year JGB yield edged up 1.5 bp to nearly 2.17%, Australia and New Zealand yields jumped 10 bp. European benchmark 10-year rates are mostly 1-2 bp higher, while the 10-year Gilt is up nearly four to 4.72%. The US 10-year Treasury yield is slightly softer, around 4.22%.

• Gold is trading quietly and remains in Tuesday’s range (~$5118-5239). Silver slipped to a three-day low slightly below $84 but recovered to session highs near $87.20 in Europe.

• April WTI gapped higher today and reached almost $96 before consolidating. It is above $91 in Europe. The market has not been swayed by the IEA announcement yesterday. It remains light on details (pace and composition). The US says it will release 172 mln barrels of oil but it will take 120 days to deliver. Even during the 2022 release following Russia’s invasion of Ukraine, the release never exceeded 1.1 mln barrels a day.

Data

• The US reports January’s trade deficit today. It likely will be around half of last January’s $128.3 bln shortfall, which was a blow-out from the $67-69 bln deficit in January 2024 and January 2023, respectively. Imports were nearly flat last year, after rising an average of 1% a month in 2024. Exports rose by an average of 0.5% a month last year and 0.3% a month on average in 2024. Weekly initial jobless claims were 213k for the past two weeks, while continuing claims reached eight-week highs in the week ending February 20 (1.868 mln). Poor weather likely weighed on January housing starts. They rose last November and December. Lastly, the US reports Q4 '25 household net worth. House prices on average slipped less than 0.5% in Q4, the 10-year yield rose slightly, though the Fed cut rates twice in Q4 '25, and the S&P 500 rose almost 2.4%. Despite the chronic current account deficit and budget deficit in excess of 5% post-Covid, US household net worth has never been higher. The issue is distribution, not wealth creation per se.

• Canada reports the January merchandise trade balance today. It recorded a C$31.3 bln goods deficit last year, swelling after a C$7.2 bln shortfall in 2024 and slightly less than a C$1 bln deficit in 2023. Canada reports February jobs data tomorrow. It is difficult to imagine a better report than January’s, which saw the unemployment rate fall to 6.5% from 6.8% and 45k full-time jobs created. However, the participation rate did slip to 65% from 65.4%, matching the low since January 2021.

• Australia’s Melbourne Institute’s consumer inflation expectations edged up to 5.2% from 5.0% in February. This is almost a three-year high, and keeps expectations elevated for a rate hike next week. There is nearly a 66% chance discounted in the futures market, up from almost 11% at the end of last month.

data.title: PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

U.S. equity markets capped a strong year of performance with modest gains in the fourth quarter. Resurging concerns about the durability of artificial intelligence (AI) development and the enormous levels of investment spent on AI infrastructure led to volatility during the last three months of the year. Despite these fears, and associated valuation worries, the Federal Reserve's easing monetary policy, moderating but still positive economic growth, and the ongoing penetration of AI buoyed the markets as the year ended.

The S&P 500 advanced by 2.7% for the quarter and, after a long stretch of outperformance by growth and large cap equities, market participation broadened out during the period. The tech-heavy Nasdaq outperformed the Index due to continued concentrated strength in mega-cap tech and AI beneficiaries. However, modest cooling in AI-led growth leadership and a rotation away from highly concentrated, premium valuation growth toward discounted cyclical and defensive sectors led to the outperformance of value stocks. Expectations of a lower interest rate environment helped support cyclicals as well as small caps during the quarter.

Trepidation around valuations left no cushion for missing earnings expectations, and even companies that met expectations, but didn't exceed them, saw their share prices decline. Most sectors lagged the modest market return, with only healthcare and communications services outperforming. The healthcare sector led the market in the fourth quarter as the group rebounded after being under intermittent pressure since late 2024. Relief on the political front, solid earnings, innovation, notable strength in obesity/diabetes franchises, as well as discounted valuations, supported its performance. While communications services and technology drove market performance for the year, technology underperformed in the fourth quarter while communications services continued its outperformance, driven by media M&A and continued strength in mega cap interactive media/internet names.

For the fourth quarter, Index performance is as follows: S&P Financials +2.0%; S&P 500 +2.7%; Russell 1000 Growth +1.1%; and Russell 1000 Value +3.8%.

Quarter

The PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund advanced and outperformed the 2.0% return of the S&P Composite 1500 Financials index for the quarter. Relative outperformance was largely driven by security selection within financial services (especially an overweight to Apollo Global Management), capital markets (led by an overweight in Goldman Sachs and an out of index position in Marex Group), and insurance driven by overweights to Chubb Limited, Lincoln National, and RenaissanceRe Holdings. On the downside, security selection within banks and out of index exposure to information technology and consumer discretionary detracted the most from relative results during the quarter.

Key Contributors

  • Goldman Sachs (GS)
  • Chubb Limited (CB)
  • Bank of America (BAC)

Goldman Sachs has a strong capital base and leading global positions in investment banking, capital markets, trading, and asset management. It is a business with broad reach and durable competitive advantages across multiple product lines. Additionally, we favor their risk management capabilities and seasoned management team. The company reported strong results for the 3rd quarter, and we continue to believe that Goldman Sachs will be a major beneficiary of corporate restructurings and Mergers and Acquisitions (M&A) activity, along with favorable credit trends.

Chubb Limited is a global provider of insurance products covering property and casualty, accident and health, reinsurance, and life insurance, and is the largest publicly traded property and casualty company in the world. The company operates in 55 countries and is a best-in-class operator with discipline around pricing and underwriting. The company reported another solid fundamental quarter, which led to the stock bouncing back from a weak 2nd quarter.

Bank of America is a financial institution, serving individual consumers, small and middle market businesses, institutional investors, large corporations, and governments with a range of banking, investing, asset management and other financial and risk management products and services. The company continued its multi-year trend of improving fundamentals (both in growth and quality). We continue to like its strong retail franchise, multiple commercial business levers for growth, and improving fee income dynamics, as well as its solid capitalization; diversified funding sources; and well-regarded senior leadership and risk management infrastructure.

Barron's: PGIM Investments ranked 4 out of 48, 8 out of 47, 12 out of 46 firms for the 1-, 5-, and 10-year periods ended 12/31/2024, respectively. See back page for methodology which takes into account Lipper rankings. PGIM Jennison Financial Services Fund (Class Z) Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2024 for the Financial Services Fund category were: 7/85, 23/82, 10/82, and 27/71, respectively. Lipper Funds category rankings are based on total return, do not take sales charges into account, and are calculated against all funds in each fund's respective Lipper category. Lipper total return ranking for the 1-, 3-, 5-, and 10-year periods as of 12/31/2025: 41/81, 11/81, 30/79, and 24/69, respectively. Past performance is no guarantee of future results.

Key Detractors

  • Houlihan Lokey (HLI)
  • Marsh & McLennan (MRSH)
  • LPL Financial (LPLA)

Houlihan Lokey is an investment banking company that provides M&A, capital market, financial restructurings and liability management, and financial and valuation advisory services worldwide. Shares were down despite better than expected third quarter results as some investors may have found future growth expectations underwhelming.

Marsh & McLennan is a professional services company providing advisory services and insurance solutions to clients in the areas of risk, strategy, and people worldwide. It operates through Risk and Insurance Services, and Consulting segments. The stock was down because of a gradual slowing in the company's consulting and risk underwriting segments. We continue to like the company's industry-leading franchise and risk management expertise at an attractive valuation.

LPL Financial is a leading and rapidly growing investment advisory firm. The firm reported disappointing results for the third quarter – specifically around guidance and the status of their integration efforts from recent acquisitions (both cost elimination and growth dynamics). We sold the position as a result.

Portfolio Outlook

We continue to see solid fundamentals across a broad range of business models in the financial sector, along with an improving global macro (primarily credit conditions and the consumer holding up well). We are also seeing ongoing improvement in inflation expectations (with the Fed easing to a new target rate of 3.75-4.0%) as we transition into 2026. Finally, the new administration's agenda and its future implications around lower taxes (especially around capex spending), less balance sheet capital requirements, and less regulation (one that is perceived as pro-business and pro-growth) has been an additional catalyst behind improving earnings growth expectations across multiple sectors and industries.

Nevertheless, there continues to be uncertainty around the future structure of the tariffs, time lags involved, responses from our trading partners, and then implications around forward confidence and sentiment for both the consumer and business. The financial sector would be negatively impacted if any of these factors turn down, specifically causing higher credit losses and slowing consumer and business lending activity.

data.title: Snowflake: Strong Growth, But Valuation Appears Fair
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome Equity Lab as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

The start of Snowflake Inc. (SNOW)’s share price into 2026 has been bumpy. Concerns that AI could eliminate business models in the IT service sector heavily impacted the company’s share price. However, the company’s actual operating performance remained strong. In addition, Snowflake remains one of the most important independent platforms in the enterprise data and AI ecosystem.

I use a simplified DCF model to show that at current valuation levels, the share price of Snowflake looks fair. Therefore, I have a neutral view on the company, as the market is already pricing in a successful execution path, and any meaningful operational missteps could have a negative impact on the valuation of the company’s share price.

Company Overview

Products and Offerings

Snowflake is a cloud-based data platform. Companies can store, process, and analyze large amounts of data across different formats. It does not matter if the data is structured (for example, data stored in tables such as financial transactions or customer records) or unstructured (such as emails, documents, images, or log files). As long as the data can be stored digitally, it can be analyzed within the Snowflake platform.

The company stands for three core elements: scalability, data governance, and ease of use. Snowflake allows customers to share data from one data source. There is no need to create multiple copies, which helps to reduce data inconsistencies (often happens when the same data exists in various locations). In addition, this data sharing can be done while keeping the necessary access controls around who is allowed to see what data. This “package” of data storage and sharing has become very important as companies integrate AI applications that depend on reliable and well-governed data.

Another advantage of Snowflake is its ease of use. While IT skills are still required, users do not need deep database or infrastructure expertise to work with the platform.

What’s more, Snowflake’s pricing model differs from the one that “classic” SaaS providers use. Snowflake charges customers based on consumption and not based on a fixed monthly subscription fee. This increases the flexibility of the platform, as customers only pay for the services they actually use. This is often more aligned with their needs.

Major Independent Platform Competitor

Databricks is the most significant independent competitor to Snowflake. The company started in data engineering and machine learning. This gave it strong credibility within data scientists. As of now, however, Databricks has expanded beyond these specialized use cases and also targets enterprise-wide analytics and AI workloads. This puts the company in direct competition with Snowflake.

Snowflake’s potential weakness is that Databricks still has stronger credibility among data scientists, especially for more advanced AI and data engineering workloads.

The way I see it, however, is that Snowflake has built much of its success around ease of use, governance features, and a platform that is accessible for everybody. And accessible, especially for non-technical users, which continues to be the perception. This is, in my opinion, a significant strength of Snowflake.

Competition From Hyperscalers

Beyond Databricks, Snowflake also competes with the hyperscalers like Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Amazon.com Inc. (AMZN), and Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL). These hyperscalers offer their own data platforms within their cloud.

One key advantage for hyperscalers is that they control the underlying infrastructure. This allows them to combine offerings on compute, storage, data, and AI services, which can create pricing leverage.

Therefore, a potential weakness for Snowflake is that hyperscalers could use their financial resources and control over the cloud infrastructure to steer customers toward their own platforms.

However, I still view the core economic priority of hyperscalers to be infrastructure utilization because the majority of their capex continues to go towards infrastructure buildout (=data centers). As long as Snowflake maintains strong product execution, hyperscalers may have limited incentive to aggressively go against it. Snowflake ultimately brings incremental workload consumption on their clouds, which is good for them.

Moreover, many customers also may not want to have all services from a single provider due to dependency concerns. In my view, this is also a key strength for Snowflake and helps explain why hyperscalers maintain partnerships with Snowflake despite competitive overlap. Snowflake’s ability to run across multiple cloud providers also supports customers who want to have a multi-cloud strategy.

News and Catalysts

Snowflake’s recent operating performance showed that demand for its services remains strong. In FY26, revenue grew approx. 29%, while non-GAAP operating profit more than doubled. On a GAAP bases, Snowflake still reports a loss, mostly due to stock-based compensation. But operating leverage continues to improve with scaling revenue.

Management also continues to project solid growth for FY27. Revenue is expected to increase approx. 27%, which was slightly more than market expectations. Therefore, demand still looks healthy, and despite the ongoing debate about AI disruption, Snowflake’s core business appears to be intact.

An additional sign of strong demand is that Snowflake managed to sign very large customer contracts. In Q4 FY26, the company signed its largest deal ever, with $400 million, and closed 7 additional nine-figure agreements. According to management, these came from existing customers expanding their usage, which suggests that companies increasingly rely on Snowflake.

Another positive development in my view is that the stock-based compensation has continued to decline as a % of revenue. As the CEO Sridhar Ramaswamy mentioned in the earnings call, stock-based compensation represented approx. 34% of revenue in FY26, down from approx. 41% in FY25, and is expected to further fall to about 27% in FY27.

Although stock-based compensation is a non-cash expense, it still matters because it creates share dilution and reduces the value per share.

Management did not provide a detailed explanation of how this reduction will be achieved. I think the most likely explanation is that revenue continues to grow faster than stock-based compensation. As Snowflake matures, hiring growth will likely moderate, which should reduce the need for large new equity grants.

Valuation

In 2025, Snowflake traded at valuation levels that implied very optimistic expectations for growth and profitability.

I use a simplified DCF model to value Snowflake. My base case assumes that revenue growth moderates as business scales and GAAP operating margins improve over time (primarily on the back of stock-based compensation, which currently explains most of the difference between GAAP and non-GAAP).

FCF is calculated from NOPAT, depreciation, maintenance capital expenditures, and working capital. Under these assumptions, I get to a long-term FCF-margin of about 26%, which is broadly in line with mature SaaS companies such as Microsoft Corporation (MSFT), Adobe Inc. (ADBE), ServiceNow, Inc. (NOW), Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), and Atlassian Corporation (TEAM), as can be seen in the graph below.

I use a terminal growth rate of 2% and a weighted average cost of capital of 8%. I have noticed that some folks use a higher terminal growth rate, but I include share dilution related to stock-based compensation (which reduces the equity value per share) in the terminal value.

Based on these assumptions, I get to a fair value range of $150 to $170 per share, depending on variations in the WACC and terminal growth assumptions.

Peer Comparison

Let’s also look at how Snowflake’s valuation compares to some of its peer companies like MongoDB Inc. (MDB), Cloudflare Inc. (NET), Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR), and Elastic N.V. (ESTC).

To simplify the comparison, I didn’t calculate a DCF model for each peer company but used the P/S (NTM) multiple for selected companies. Revenue multiples remain a common valuation metric for high-growth software companies because profitability often lags revenue growth for many years.

The chart shows, Palantir trades at a higher multiple than the other companies in the group. This is likely because of strong investor enthusiasm around its positioning in the AI market. Cloudflare also trades at a relatively high valuation due to its strong growth profile. In contrast, MongoDB and Elastic trade at lower multiples. Snowflake currently trades approx. in the middle of this peer group.

While I don’t claim that this is a comprehensive peer list, I think it still gives a useful reference point. Snowflake currently trades about in the middle of this group. In my view, this is further evidence that the indication from my simplified DCF model holds and that Snowflake is currently about fairly valued.

Risk to my valuation

There are some factors that could significantly alter my investment thesis.

First, competition from hyperscalers could intensify if they decide to integrate their own data platforms more aggressively. This would make it difficult for Snowflake to offer its services at similar competitive prices as potentially the hyperscalers could offer.

Second, revenue growth could slow much faster than expected as the company scales. If operating margins fail to expand as assumed in the model, the current valuation could become difficult to justify.

Third, the DCF model heavily depends on the discount rate. I assume that the risk-free rate stays at around current levels. If the risk-free rate were to significantly increase (for example, due to accelerating inflation), this would have a significant negative impact on Snowflake’s valuation.

Conclusion

Competition in the data platform market is intensifying. However, the addressable market is, in my opinion, large enough to support multiple successful platforms. A winner-takes-it-all outcome looks unlikely to me.

The key question is less about demand and AI disruption and more about execution. Snowflake has to demonstrate that it can translate its strong revenue growth into positive non-adjusted margins. And this is also on a GAAP level and not just on adjusted figures!

I view Snowflake as well positioned within the growing enterprise data and AI ecosystem. Recent AI-related fears look overstated to me. I think that the importance of a platform that is well-governed, scalable, and easy to use plays directly to Snowflake’s strengths.

At the current price, however, the stock appears broadly fairly valued under my base case assumptions. Upside from here would likely require either stronger-than-expected margin expansion or a longer period of elevated growth. The latter I see as less likely. I have a “neutral” view on the company and would not add to the position unless the share price drops to $150 or lower.

data.title: Royce Small-Cap Value Fund FY 2025: What Worked... And What Didn't
data.description:
The following segment was excerpted from the Royce Small-Cap Value Fund FY 2025 Commentary.

Seven of the portfolio’s 10 equity sectors made a positive impact on calendar year performance. Information Technology, Financials, and Industrials made the largest positive contributions. The biggest negative impacts came from Energy, Consumer Discretionary, and Real Estate.

At the industry level, electronic equipment, instruments & components (Information Technology), banks (Financials), and commercial services & supplies (Industrials) contributed most in 2025, while oil, gas & consumable fuels (Energy), specialty retail (Consumer Discretionary), and ground transportation (Industrials) were the largest detractors.

Three of the Fund’s top five contributors provide electronics contract manufacturing services, or “EMS.” This business continues to experience robust demand—with many of the stocks being re-rated—because of their close involvement building AI data centers.

A top contributor from 2024, Sanmina Corporation (SANM) offers these services to a global customer base. The company bought ZT Systems, a data center manufacturing business, from semiconductor behemoth Advanced Micro Devices (AMD) while also entering into a strategic partnership with AMD. As a result of the acquisition, Sanmina expects to “strengthen its leading end-to-end component technology, systems integration, and supply chain solutions.” Jabil (JBL) provides EMS and solutions for several industries including automotive and transportation, capital equipment, and consumer companies. The company has enjoyed vibrant growth in its end markets, especially data center infrastructure, cloud computing, and solar energy, all of which helped Jabil to post better-than-expected earnings and raised guidance in 2025. Flex (FLEX) is an EMS company that designs and develops original design manufacturing (ODM) products for several industries, including aerospace & defense, cloud computing, digital health, lighting, housing, energy, industrial, and communications. Flex reported record adjusted operating margins for both fiscal 4Q25 and the full year in May of 2025, delivering the company’s fifth consecutive year of double-digit adjusted EPS (earnings per share) growth. Recent growth has come largely from AI data center components as well as from cloud computing and medical devices.

Sterling Infrastructure (STRL) is a construction services company that specializes in infrastructure projects. Sterling generates more than 50% of its profits from site preparation for mission-critical construction jobs such as data centers, which account for roughly 60% of its current backlog. The company’s scale (it’s five times larger than its next biggest competitor) and brand equity, along with the low cost but critical importance of site prep to the overall economic success of a construction project, generate customer lock-in. Sterling often has negative working capital thanks to favorable customer funding terms in this core site prep business while its legacy highway infrastructure segment provides excess cash flow which management has adeptly reinvested to fund high ROIC organic growth and M&A in higher margin businesses that broaden its capabilities. Sterling has also been benefiting from favorable secular tailwinds driving overall growth, including data center buildouts, reshoring, the entry-level housing supply gap, and infrastructure spending. Its shares jumped more than 104% in 2025 thanks to its strong and growing backlog, along with incremental industry datapoints that bolstered the case for continued growth in data center construction over the next few years. Additionally, the company announced the acquisition of an electrical contracting manufacturer for a reasonable multiple and that aligns perfectly with management’s strategic intent of adding additional services and building out its presence in the Southwest.

IBEX (IBEX) delivers tech-enabled customer-service solutions and business process outsourcing for digital marketing, sales and support, and brand management. In February, management reported record quarterly revenue that was generated by new customer wins and expanding business with existing clients, with most of the success rooted in AI-related services. More positive news came in November, when IBEX reported broad-based growth, improved profitability, and strong earnings. The company also raise guidance for fiscal 2026. Growth was particularly strong in its Retail & E-commerce segment, while HealthTech and Travel/Transportation/Logistics also showed impressive results.

Two of the Fund’s top detractors in 2025 were energy companies. Oil and natural gas producer SM Energy (SM) drills mostly in the Midland, South Texas, and Uinta Basins. Its stock fell sharply during 1Q25, partly due to tumbling commodity prices and more specifically due to a hefty increase in production costs associated with the Uinta Basin acquisition, which overwhelmed a 36% increase in SM’s total average net daily equivalent production year-over-year for the same period. Civitas Resources focuses on developing and producing crude oil, natural gas, and natural gas liquids in Colorado’s Denver-Julesburg Basin and the Permian Basin. The company closed on two acquisitions over the last two years that added leverage to its balance sheet, which helped keep investors away from its stock. The shares of both holdings remained sluggish when it was announced in November that SM Energy would be acquiring Civitas in all-stock deal for virtually no premium. If the synergies and cost savings from the merger materialize as forecasted, SM Energy’s stock should rebound as production levels remain solid.

J. Jill (JILL) retails women’s apparel, accessories, and footwear. Potential tariffs and declines in consumer spending were largely to blame for the stock’s downward trajectory in 2025. However, we like the new CEO’s long-term strategy and prospects for the brand, which has evolved from catalogue only to a mix of online, catalogue, and brick and mortar. In 2025, management upgraded the company’s order management system to improve returns and logistics, invested $10.3 million in cloud-based software to bolster its digital operations, and added three new stores, giving J. Jill a total of 247 locations.

Two footwear retailers were among the Fund’s five biggest detractors in 2025: Shoe Carnival (SCVL) offers casual and athletic footwear for men, women, and children under well-known brands including Skechers, Clarks, Adidas, Crocs, New Balance, Converse, Roxy, Nike, and Vans. Its shares began to recover in May off their April lows, though not enough to stay out of the red through the remainder of 2025. As is the case with J. Jill, we think the company has managed the business effectively during a highly challenging period for many retailers, including the start of a rebranding of most of its stores to Shoe Station, which retails a broader and higher-end selection than Shoe Carnival itself. In March, management reported better-than-expected earnings on lower-than-anticipated revenues for fiscal 2024. Shoe Carnival then reported fiscal 1Q25 results in May that included higher-than-expected profits. This did little to reverse the downward trend, however, as net sales remained tepid, and operating income fell.

We were less confident in the long-term prospects for Caleres (CAL), which provides athletic, casual, and dress footwear products, operating through the Famous Footwear and Brand Portfolio segments. Famous Footwear offers branded footwear for the entire family while the Brand Portfolio—which includes Allen Edmonds, Dr. Scholl’s, and Vince—sources, manufactures, and markets branded, licensed, and private-labeled footwear primarily to online retailers, national chains, department stores, mass merchandisers, and independent retailers. The inflationary pressures on lower-income shoppers continued to crimp sales in the Famous Footwear segment, helping to lead the stock downward. We sold the last of our shares in July.

The Fund’s underperformance versus the Russell 2000 Value in 2025 came from stock selection; sector allocation decisions were positive. At the sector level, stock selection and, to a lesser extent, higher exposure in Consumer Discretionary and Energy detracted the most, followed by a combination of stock picking and a lower weighting in Materials. Conversely, stock selection and our larger weighting in Information Technology, a much lower weighting in Real Estate, and stock selection and a lower weighting in Financials were all additive relative to the benchmark.

Fund Performance

Royce Small-Cap Value Fund advanced 6.7% in 2025 versus a 12.6% gain for its benchmark, the Russell 2000 Value Index, for the same period. The Fund outperformed its benchmark for the 5-year, and since inception (6/14/01) periods ended 12/31/25.

What Worked… and What Didn’t

Seven of the portfolio’s 10 equity sectors made a positive impact on calendar year performance. Information Technology, Financials, and Industrials made the largest positive contributions. The biggest negative impacts came from Energy, Consumer Discretionary, and Real Estate.

Top Contributors to Performance For 20251

Sanmina Corporation
Sterling Infrastructure
IBEX
Flex
Jabil

1Includes dividends

Top Detractors from Performance For 20252

SM Energy
J. Jill
Civitas Resources
Shoe Carnival
Caleres

2Net of dividends

Current Positioning and Outlook

The rally that boosted small-caps off the April lows was dominated by low-quality and more speculative companies, and left the kind of conservatively capitalized, quality businesses we focus on trailing. The most notable change in sector weights was bringing the Fund’s Health Care exposure close to the benchmark’s weighting for the first time in many years and finishing 2025 overweight in pharmaceuticals. Otherwise, sector weights did not change appreciably. The portfolio remained overweight in Consumer Discretionary and Information Technology, slightly overweight in Financials (and in banks, by far the sector’s largest industry weighting) and close to the benchmark in Energy. The market is currently in a peculiar place. The AI frenzy may well be a bubble as mega-cap companies keep passing enormous sums of money back and forth to each other—which looks to us like a strong warning sign. In addition, stocks as a whole are not cheap. Spring will bring a new Fed Chair, one who will presumably be amenable to further rate cuts. However, if the Fed continues lower rates because they want to protect employment, it’s likely to spur more inflation—and no one really knows how much of this the market may already be pricing in. Further complicating matters is the fact that lower-income households remain under severe pressure, which may get worse for people who are not poor enough for Medicaid but rely on ACA subsidies that are going away. This has created a challenging investment environment made more even more difficult by the state of equity valuations—which leaves less room for error if the economy slows or if the geopolitical situation gets hotter than it already is. We continue to watch for greater clarity and stability in both the market and economy.

data.title: What Warren Buffett Saw In Occidental Petroleum -- And Why The Thesis Still Holds
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Occidental Petroleum (OXY) is a best-in-class Permian operator whose proprietary CO2 enhanced oil recovery techniques offer a durable technical edge over peers. The company's recently streamlined balance sheet following the OxyChem divestiture leaves the company well-positioned to generate meaningful free cash flow at conservative oil prices. At a long-run assumption of $75/barrel, owner earnings of approximately $5.30/share imply an intrinsic value of $40–$45, a figure that does not require an optimistic commodity outlook and embeds substantial optionality from rising EOR recovery rates and DAC carbon credit sales. The primary risk is a structural oil supply glut driven by new South American production, but investors with a multi-year horizon and comfort with commodity exposure are buying Berkshire(BRK.B)-backed management with a technologically-advanced producer focused on the prolific Permian basin.

Business Model

Oxy is the third largest domestic oil & gas producer with a big position in the Permian basin. The company is unique in their advanced EOR (enhanced oil recovery) procedures in which they employ proprietary CO2 flooding techniques, which push successive waves of water and CO2 through porous shale rock to improve recovery rates. In conventional (meaning non-shale) wells oil companies typically get ~75% of oil out of the reservoir using CO2 flooding, but that number stands at just 10% in shale. Oxy is the industry leader in this practice, and as one of the largest holders of non-conventional acreage in America, it stands to benefit from continued advances in this technology.

Oxy’s production is based primarily in the US (84% of 2025 MBoe), with domestic revenue split ~85%/10%/5% between oil/NGLs/natural gas. US-based production mostly comes from the Permian (65%), with the remainder from the DJ basin (24%) and Gulf of America (11%).

International production splits looks similar, with 2025 sales split between 80% oil, 10% NGLs, and 10% natural gas from projects in Oman, the UAE, and Oman.

Bottom line – Oxy’s financial performance is largely dependent on their American production, specifically from the Permian. One disadvantage of Permian production is the relatively low in-basin price of oil and gas, with the Waha basis commonly pulling the price of natural gas below $0. Oxy’s midstream segment is designed to alleviate this local oversupply by transporting product to US refineries and to the gulf for export.

Oxy captures these transportation spreads (Midland-to-GC oil and Waha-to-GC gas) through their marketing group (housed within the midstream segment). This part of the business purchases pipeline capacity to the gulf and optimizes the utilization with their own product and by purchasing from third parties. Oxy doesn’t explicitly own much pipeline takeaway capacity beyond their own gathering systems.

Much of the physical midstream infrastructure Oxy does own is wrapped up in their 43% ownership of a publicly traded MLP – WES – which owns ~15k miles of pipeline plus associated treatment/compression facilities. WES distributed about $600 million to Oxy in 2025.

The physical midstream infrastructure Oxy explicitly owns is focused on wet-gas treatment and CO2 extraction/delivery. Oxy’s facilities purify the gas stream coming out of wells to produce pipeline-quality gas, extract NGLS, and – unique to Oxy – capture CO2 for delivery back to wells to be used in continuing CO2 flooding operations.

Oxy Low Carbon Ventures (OLCV) is also housed within the midstream segment and is in the process of developing direct-air CO2 capture (DAC) technologies. The STRATOS DAC facility is expected to begin capturing and sequestering CO2 this year and will eventually generate revenue from carbon removal credit sales to customers like JP Morgan (JPM) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW). The appeal of DAC is apparent given Oxy’s CO2 expertise, but this has been a cost center for several years and I do not factor prospective earnings from this venture in my analysis.

Recent Developments

To understand Oxy, you need to understand the series of acquisitions and divestitures that have led to today’s form. Oxy purchased Anadarko for $55 billion in 2019 with backing from Berkshire Hathaway in the form of $10 billion in preferred stock (with an 8% yield) plus warrants to purchase 83.9 million shares at ~$60/share. Ironically – the company’s market cap today is roughly equivalent to the price they paid for Anadarko.

Still, Berkshire’s backing was a strong vote of confidence, which was important as Oxy significantly levered their balance sheet. Between 2018 and 2019 LT debt jumped from $10 billion to over $38 billion and the share count was significantly diluted as a result of Berkshire’s warrants (diluted shares outstanding are up about 20% from 2018 to today). The company sold off its African assets to Total along with some other non-core American acreage to bring down their heavy debt load.

While this massive acquisition at a high price initial smacks of empire building, Warren Buffett’s comments on the transaction are reassuring:

In the case of Occidental specifically, they'd had an analyst presentation... I read it over a weekend. And what Vicki Hollub was saying made nothing but sense. And I decided that it was a good place to put Berkshire's money... Vicki was saying what the company had gone through and where it was now and what they planned to do with the money... But we decided it made sense. And two weeks later, we had 14% of the company.

Since that transaction, Oxy has made two additional major transactions with the same strategic focus – a $12 billion acquisition of Permian E&P Crown Rock and the divestiture of their OxyChem unit to Berkshire for $9.7 billion. Oxy had reduced their debt load to ~$17 billion in 2023 when they announced the Crown Rock acquisition, but obviously had to increase leverage to swallow the new addition. Their recent Oxychem divestiture gets the company where it originally wanted to be debt-wise (around $15 billion with no significant maturities through 2029) with a focused portfolio of Permian acreage where the company feels like they have a significant advantage through their EOR techniques.

Earnings Power and Valuation

So where does this series of transactions leave them now? The table below shows recent results and my 2026 estimate of production and earnings power.

My production assumptions for 2026 are based on a combination of Q4, 2025 daily production rates and management guidance for 2026. I've projected an average sales price of $75/barrel for oil, $18/barrel for NGLs and $1.60/Mcf for natural gas. Oxy’s marketing team uses midstream capacity to get average sales prices as close to market prices as possible, but the company is more focused on oil than natural gas, which explains why their historical sales price for oil is closer to the WTI index than natural gas is to Henry Hub. My calculation of owner earnings is simply net income + DD&A – capex, which conservatively includes growth capex.

The resulting earnings power of ~$5.30/share (which includes additional dilution from Berkshire exercising all warrants) should be fairly conservative in that it doesn’t incorporate significant production increases, much growth in oil/gas prices, or any benefit from DAC sales and the ramp-down of capex as the Stratos DAC facility nears completion in 2026.

Under these assumptions, with earnings growth of just 3%/year in perpetuity, the value of Oxy’s owner earnings discounted at a rate of 15% (representing my opportunity cost) are around $40-$45. This leaves significant upside in the form of higher commodity prices, CO2 sequestration sales, and improved recovery rates from Oxy’s world-class EOR techniques. Furthermore, the recent spike in oil prices from the war in Iran shows how Oxy may benefit from an increasingly turbulent world.

Risks

The biggest risk to an investment in Oxy is a structural oversupply in the oil market. Prior to the Iranian war, the world was awash in oil. That dynamic has been temporarily broken by the current state in the Strait of Hormuz, but it should fundamentally keep a lid on oil prices over the next several years. New discoveries in Brazil, Guyana, and Argentina along with expanded production in Venezuela may eventually lessen the impact from middle-eastern turbulence and eventually worsen the supply glut. For that reason, it’s probably best not to bank on sustained high commodity prices as an investment thesis for Oxy, despite the current state of affairs.

Another risk of investing in Oxy is further share dilution from large acquisitions. Vicki Hollob has shown that she’s not scared of making large purchases, obtaining financing by any means necessary. Further high-priced acquisitions combined with low oil & gas prices would be a poor environment for future shareholder returns.

Finally, in a capital-intensive operation such as Oxy’s, higher interest rates present a significant risk. As mentioned above, the company has no major debt maturities before 2029, which counteracts this risk in the near-term, but may impact them as debt rolls over into the 2030s.

Summary

Occidental Petroleum is a high-quality Permian operator with a differentiated position in CO2-enhanced oil recovery, a strategically focused asset base following its Anadarko and Crown Rock acquisitions, and a now-cleaned-up balance sheet after the OxyChem divestiture. Investors comfortable with commodity price volatility and willing to own a leveraged, Permian-concentrated E&P are buying a company with genuine technical advantages, Berkshire’s continued backing, and a valuation that does not require an optimistic oil price outlook to justify.

I’m personally interested in become a long-term shareholder, but will be staying on the sidelines for now. The company was trading within my target price range in the low-$40s prior to the Iran war, which has catapulted the company’s stock price well above $50. If the war resolves within the next few weeks (as seems likely), Oxy’s stock price may drift back down into a range I’m more comfortable buying.

data.title: A Prudent Investor Watches What Insiders Do, Not What They Say
data.description:
The market has been hit by elevated volatility here in March due to the conflict in Iran which has taken nearly 20% of global oil supplies offline. However, every dip in equities has been bought and the major indexes are not that far off from their all-time highs.

"Whoever is careless with the truth in small matters cannot be trusted with important matters” ― Albert Einstein

This leaves stocks still trading at extreme valuation levels, using numerous historical valuation metrics. I have always been a proponent of judging an individual by their actions, not their words. And prudent investors should be watching the 'insiders' closely for clues on where markets might head from here. In today's article, I will highlight some observations I have on corporate insiders and managers of investor funds over the past couple of months.

We will start with some positives. Kudos to the management of KKR & Co. (KKR). The CEO and a couple of insiders have gobbled up just over $35 million in new equity purchases in recent weeks. The company has seen some of the negative headlines as brethren Blue Owl Capital (OWL), BlackRock (BLK) and Jefferies Financial Group (JEF), all of which have been hit by deteriorating conditions in the credit markets. Nonetheless, the stock is down 40% from its highs late last summer. The insider buying is one of few signs of conviction I have seen from insiders in recent months.

In addition, one has to give credit to Blackstone (BX). Company managers threw in $150 million of their own money and the firm offered up $250 million of its own capital to allow redemptions of 7.9% at one large fund instead of the stated five percent quarterly cap last week.

This action contrast positively to those of Blue Owl Capital and BlackRock which have had some high profiled 'gating' of investor redemption requests in recent weeks, which have sent ripples across the credit markets. And the environment for the private credit space just has continued to deteriorate this week.

Wednesday, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) ⁠disclosed Wednesday that it has marked ​down ‌the value ‌of ⁠certain loans ⁠held by private-credit groups. The huge bank also is tightening credit and limiting funding to the sector. It also became known Wednesday that Cliffwater was seeing higher than seven percent redemption requests around its flagship $33 billion private credit fund.

Also, on Wednesday, the president at PIMCO, which has $2.3 trillion AUM, did his best Captain Obvious impression and stated that 'the rising strain in the private credit market is a result of years of sloppy underwriting standards in lending'. Which is a bit like closing the barn door after most of the horses have escaped. All of this tells me is we are going to see many more 'cockroaches' in the private credit markets in the months and quarters ahead.

Before the 'SaaSpocalypse' along with growing worries around AI disruption and new concerns around private credit in February and a new conflict in the Middle East, insiders were growing wearier around market valuations. The insider selling/buying ratio hit its highest levels in five years during January. Of course, this was happening just as the retail investor was pushing margin debt to all-time highs.

Finally, just as private credit funds are facing spiking redemption requests, some well-known billionaires are trying to go public so they can sell stakes in their firms at heightened valuations. Bill Ackman's Pershing Square is seeking to go public. Elon Musk's SpaceX is reportedly exploring an IPO in mid-2026 at a valuation of up to $1.5 trillion. This would be the largest IPO in history.

If this IPO actually gets executed, it well could be like ringing the bell at the top. It will be a fascinating development to watch. In the meantime, I will continue to monitor the 'tea leaves' coming from investors. And right now, they are reinforcing my cautious view on the overall markets.

data.title: Accenture: Undervalued After Years Of Overvaluation
data.description:
Co-authored by Kody's Dividends

How easy is it for you to forgive somebody?

I mean truly forgive someone, as in forgive them and move on without constantly mentioning it or using it as ammo over their head. If we were honest with ourselves, the vast majority of us really struggle with forgiveness. We're good at saying the words. We're bad at living it out afterward. We like to hold grudges, to constantly bring up past failures to beat down someone who's trying to move forward, or use those past failures as leverage to force this person to do something they don't want to do.

When it comes to investing, most investors are no better. There is a reason why the dividend cutters have statistically worse performance over the long run than those companies that initiate dividends or grow dividends. It's because investors are unforgiving. Aside from maybe some high-yield investors who see dividend cuts regularly and continue to buy or recommend the same companies over and over. Most investors are not willing to play with fire when it comes to their capital.

What happens if a company hasn't been cutting its dividend, but just has become extremely expensive? Investors shouldn't just look at dividend growth and dividend yield to determine the value of a company. They need to look at what the company has traded hands for historically versus what it's trading hands for now. Investors can often get frustrated when they buy an overvalued company, and then that company's value returns from the sky back to earth. Many investors struggle with what I call "Icarus syndrome." I'll release an article in the future specifically discussing what this is. But in short, Icarus Syndrome is when we fly too high, whether we're buying too high of yields or too high valuations, and we suffer the consequences when we fall back to Earth.

As an investor with decades of investment time left on my horizon and with most of my readers being investors with decades of time left on their horizon, whether they're in the midst of their retirement, which can be upwards of 30 years long if you live to a standard lifespan, or whether they're still working and saving for retirement, which means they have even more time on the clock, buying overvalued securities can guarantee underperformance in the long run. When a company is no longer overvalued, it becomes time to reacquaint yourself with it.

So let's get familiar with the company that has finally come back to Earth.

A Dividend Aristocrat In The Making

Employing over 780,000 people and serving more than 9,000 clients in 120 countries, Accenture (ACN) is the world's largest professional services firm. The company handles critical business processes for clients, including marketing and sales, human resources, supply chain, and finance and accounting.

ACN's industry dominance is built on its top 10 ecosystem partners, which generated more than 60% of total revenue in Q1 2026. That includes hyperscalers (AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud) and enterprise platforms (SAP, Oracle, Salesforce, and ServiceNow). These strategic alliances allow it to co-create solutions rather than just resell them.

ACN is committed to keeping its lead, too. According to CFO Angie Park's opening remarks during the Q1 2026 Earnings Call, the company expects to allocate about $3 billion to acquisitions in FY 2026. In January, ACN announced the latest deal: an agreement to acquire Faculty, a leading UK-based AI native services and products business. Upon closing, this deal will expand ACN's capabilities to help clients reinvent core business processes with secure AI solutions that drive tangible outcomes.

The company's low cost of capital and healthy balance sheet are what support these acquisitions. To this point, ACN enjoys an AA- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook.

Financially, the company is a cash flow machine ($2.3 billion in Q1 2026 share repurchases and $1 billion of dividends paid). In Q1 2026, ACN posted $2.2 billion in advanced AI bookings. That was a blistering 76% year-over-year growth rate. This proved that while discretionary consulting is slowing, the AI pivot is pushing adjusted operating margins higher (+30 basis points YOY to 17.0% in Q1 2026).

Analysts are upbeat, too. The FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for adjusted EPS to compound by 8.2% annually through FY 2028, off a FY 2025 base of $12.93.

ACN's 3.4% dividend yield is also the highest it has been in over a decade. Better yet, the adjusted EPS payout ratio is set to be in the mid- to high-40% range for FY 2026. That provides it with plenty of flexibility to keep handing out 10% annual dividend hikes for the foreseeable future (for a Chowder number of approximately 13). This would enable ACN to build on what's already a 15-year dividend growth streak.

ACN's shares are arguably a compelling value right now. At the current $201 share price, the stock is priced at a forward 12-month P/E ratio of just 15.2x. Put into perspective, this is far below both the FAST Graphs 20-year average of 21.3 and the 10-year average of 25.7.

We would argue that a rational fair value P/E ratio for ACN is 20. That's because its 8.2% annual adjusted EPS growth consensus isn't too far off the 10-year CAGR of 10.4%.

This suggests that ACN's growth profile is largely intact. Taking out recent years (2021 through 2024) that inflated the stock's valuation multiple, we believe that our fair value multiple properly considers the risks. This indicates shares of ACN are trading at a 33% discount to our $280 fair value per share estimate.

If the company lives up to growth projections and reverts to fair value, it could post a 49% total return through February 2027. By the end of FY 2031, ACN could deliver 17% annual total returns.

Of course, ACN isn't without its risks. Still, we think these are more than priced in at current valuation levels. Key risks for ACN include an inability to attract/retain top-notch talent and a pause on digital transformation projects.

The company's continued success largely depends on its ability to keep its employees with market-leading skills and capabilities. If ACN's corporate culture can't hire or upskill employees to keep pace with the industries it serves and technological changes, it may not deliver the services needed to fulfill client demand. That could materially alter its growth potential.

Additionally, many enterprises are pausing multi-year enterprise resource planning or cloud migration projects that were once considered essential. This is based on the idea that AI-native versions could soon be available that do it cheaper.

ACN is responding to this environment by bundling smaller and faster AI projects into a broader strategic narrative to keep contract values high. In the meantime, that has at least temporarily pressured its growth. The contrarian take, though, is that these spending freezes can't persist forever because companies will eventually realize that they can't scale AI on decades-old data foundations.

Conclusion

ACN has fallen back to Earth because of the SaaS Apocalypse and surrounding growth sell-off. Yet as a fundamental business, ACN is extremely healthy. Not only can they reward shareholders with growing dividends that are now at a decade-high yield compared to their past, but they're also actively buying back their shares. The deeper ACN falls, the larger impact its share repurchases will be able to have, the more shares they're able to repurchase. Any EPS growth will be magnified due to the limited number of shareholders that remain thereafter. Share buybacks do not create new value inside the company. They create value for each individual shareholder who remains thereafter. And I like share buybacks as an effective means to reward shareholders so long as the company is doing it at an attractive value and isn't actively working on issuing new shares in the near term. There's no point in buying back your shares here if you're just going to issue new shares tomorrow. However, if you're able to buy back shares from excess cash flow while simultaneously growing your dividend, enabling you to have more dividend growth in the future, it's a win-win for everyone involved.

When it comes to being a dividend investor? Who is focused on buying strong value proposition opportunities and growing dividends? It's important to be willing to let a company come off the bench once it's no longer overvalued. What we've done today is take a look at ACN, which has been on the bench for being overvalued for a long time, but now has fallen back into an attractive price range once more to be able to provide strong total returns and a rebound as the market turns. AI is not going away. AI is likely not going to destroy everything that you know, just like personal computers didn't. But AI is going to rewrite some industries and boost productivity in other places, and ACN is an investment that allows you to enjoy the rewards of that future. Those rewards can help build your financial fortress to new strengths and new heights.

Become a Dividend King. Sit in your dividend castle.

data.title: February CPI Inflation: Steady State Can Anchor Future Rise
data.description:
As CPI inflation stays steady at both the headline and core level for February, at any other time the release would be no news. But with a war underway, the focus is not on what has happened, but what's coming.

Higher prices in March are a given, going by the recent oil price shock. It's clear that they leave no room for monetary easing later this month. Stock markets are understandably bearish too. But there are still investing opportunities around. All of this is discussed below.

What the February CPI inflation report says

CPI inflation remained unchanged from January at 2.4% YoY and core inflation remained at 2.5% YoY as well. While there's zero development as a result from last month, these are positive outcomes anyway in that they provide some room for an inflation increase without causing any particular economic disruption. Consider their context as follows:

  • The headline figure for January 2026 was at a nine month low, which means that the level is sustained in the latest read.
  • Both headline and core inflation figures are lower than the average monthly inflation over the past 12 months of 2.6% YoY and 2.8% YoY respectively.
  • The figures are also exactly at the Fed's target rate for PCE inflation for 2026.

Why the inflation increase can be muted

In any case, the inflation increase might be rather muted even as Brent crude futures have more than doubled since the start of the year. Here's how:

  • Oil prices seen subsiding: The US's EIA has upped its fuel price forecasts recently, with the rise in Brent spot price expected to be 37% bigger (see table below) to USD 79/bbl now. However, with Brent trading at over USD 90/bbl right now, the updated forecast clearly expects that the oil price spike won't sustain. In fact, the EIA says that it expects Brent's price to drop to USD 70/bbl by the end of 2026.
  • Base effect on oil price forecast: In 2025, crude price averaged at USD 69/bbl, while the initial forecast for 2026 put the number 16% lower at USD 58/bbl. As a result, while the forecast has now jumped by 37%, compared to last year, the expected increase is now a smaller 15%. Similarly, retail gas price is now expected to rise by 7.7% compared to a 6.1% drop forecast earlier. This is an important distinction to note since inflation is observed on a YoY basis.
  • Limited impact of crude price increase: One rule of thumb to assess the impact of crude price rise on inflation is that a USD 10/bbl uptick in crude price can increase inflation by 0.2 percentage points. Now, with the average increase in Brent's price expected to be just USD 10/bbl YoY, the uptick in inflation wouldn't wind up being that big. Even considering that higher gas prices and second round effects of fuel prices would also lend upward pressure on inflation, it's safe to assume that the increase won't be more than 0.5 percentage points. So, if inflation were to average at 2.4% otherwise, it can now rise to 2.9%. This is just slightly higher than the 2.7% average level seen in 2025.

No rate cuts in sight

It's clear though, that the inflation figure would get farther away from the Fed's comfort level of 2%. As a result, if the reserve stays true to form, it's going to turn hawkish once again in its FOMC statement due next week.

After all, fuel prices still remain elevated and the Fed has said more than once that inflation expectations need to be anchored (see here and here). As fuel prices recede, it might have more room for dovishness. But for now, it's little wonder that the markets see less than 1% chance of a rate cut (see chart below).

With economic projections due to release with the FOMC statement as well, however, we could anticipate an increase in inflation expectations. The Fed's expected policy rate for the year could change in tandem, which will give insight on what to expect over the remainder of the year.

Negative market response

All three key stock indices, the S&P 500 (SP500, SPX, SPY), the NASDAQ 100 Index (NDX) (QQQ) and the Dow Jones Industrial Average Index (DJI) corrected in yesterday's session (see table below). This was unlikely a response to the inflation report, though. It was more likely an ongoing response to the geopolitical situation underway. It's worth highlighting here that the S&P 500 has corrected in five of the eight sessions in March so far.

Where to invest now

So, right now we have a situation where markets are diffident and inflation is expected to rise just enough to keep policy rates unchanged. At the same time, rise in prices is unlikely to be high enough to cripple the economy. This indicates that cyclical equity sectors like financial services and consumer discretionary, which are the worst impacted YTD by 8.6% and 4.2% respectively (see chart below), could well have overcorrected.

I believe it's exactly these stocks that can see an uptick if the market environment begins to improve, as would be expected if the situation in the Middle East calms down. So, here are a few investing ideas:

  • Cyclical sector ETFs: First, the sectors' ETFs can be considered, with some care though, as they don't have unanimous Buy ratings. The State Street Financial Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLF) gets a Buy rating from Seeking Alpha analysts but is rated Sell by Quant Rating. The State Street Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Income ETF (XLY), gets an underwhelming Hold rating from both Seeking Alpha analysts and Quant Rating.
  • Financial services stocks: The biggest five holdings under XLF, all get Buy ratings from Seeking Alpha analysts as well as Wall Street. Four of these, JP Morgan Chase & Co (JPM), Visa (V), Mastercard (MA) and Bank of America (BAC) have also seen corrections of over 10%+ each YTD. These could be good stocks to consider buying on the dip.
  • Consumer discretionary stocks: For consumer discretionary stocks, I like Royal Caribbean Cruises (RCL), which is among the top 10 holdings in XLY. It's rated Buy by both Seeking Alpha and Wall Street analysts, which also coincides with my rating. Amazon (AMZN), the biggest holding in XLY, also gets a Buy rating from analysts, but I'm less certain of a potential uptick, at least in the short-term, having downgraded it to Hold recently.

What next?

If the current expectations of a softening in fuel prices over the rest of the year come to pass, inflation could stay largely under control going by its recent stability. This in turn, can improve the market's mood as well, resulting in a rotation back to cyclical equity sectors like financial services and consumer discretionary.

It could be a good time then to buy high quality cyclical stocks. Even if the situation worsens for now, over time, they have a good chance of bouncing back.

data.title: Return Of The Pawn Shop - Ezcorp And The Cascading Credit Cycle
data.description:
EZCORP Overview and Market Opportunity

EZCORP (EZPW) is an owner and operator of 1,383 pawn shops in the U.S. and Latin America, offering consumers access to short-term cash by using personal items as collateral. The company makes money through interest payments on loans made to consumers and the eventual sale of collateralized items that went unclaimed. EZCORP has become more favorable among consumers facing higher interest rates and credit issues, as the company does not require credit checks, bank accounts, and/or other verifications. More recently, EZCORP has engaged in strategic transactions to expand its pawn store offerings. EZCORP acquired Founders One, which adds 105 stores operating under the "La Familia Pawn and Jewelry" and "CashWiz" brands across 12 countries.

Q1 FY 2026 Earnings Report

In Q1 FY 2026, EZCORP reported total revenues of $374.5M, compared to $320M in Q1 FY 2025. Much of this increase was attributed to higher scrap, merchandise sales, and pawn service charges (PSC). While the rise of precious metal prices had bolstered company revenues, EZCORP's Latin America growth has also been very impressive. Management reported an 18% YoY increase in PSC, driven by same-store PLO growth and new stores.

The company also reported EBITDA of $70.3M, up 36% YoY, with an EBITDA margin of 19%, increasing by 260 bps YoY. This strong performance has bolstered EZCORP's balance sheet; short-term cash & cash equivalents as of December 31, 2025 were ~$466M. This totally ignores the company's acquisition of Founders One, as the transaction closed after the balance sheet date. However, I don't expect management to slow its acquisition strategy as it looks to use idle cash to grow its Latin American pawn markets, which have seen stronger growth than U.S. markets.

EZCORP Buy Thesis

While many investors may think that the recent price spike in the precious metals market is the only catalyst to EZCORP, I see a broader unfolding of a worsening credit environment and strategic M&A as remaining the key catalysts to continued stock outperformance. The company currently trades at a ~9.68x EV/EBITDA multiple. Additionally, EZCORP's momentum largely parallels its strength during the 2008 financial crisis.

EZCORP's shares have increased from strong revenue growth through store expansion and increased precious metal transactions, but remain below 2008 levels. The current credit environment for consumers remains very expensive, with the U.S. commercial bank interest rate on credit cards at ~21%, which is well above the 14.5% from peak 2008 levels. As the unemployment rate continues to rise, I expect demand for alternative credit instruments, which include short-term loans by pawning items, to increase.

Deteriorating Consumer Credit Supporting Pawn Shop Cycle

Despite a growing economy and strong stock market gains in recent years, the consumer credit environment continues to worsen, with delinquency rates on loans (including CCs and mortgages) rising to 4.8% of all outstanding US household debt in Q4 2025. Credit card loans were even worse, with 12.7% of these outstanding loans listed as 90-day delinquent, which is the most since Q1 2011. With a growing unemployment rate and stickier inflation more likely, I don't see these delinquency rates improving soon.

While total debt in default remains well below 2008 levels, EZCORP shares remain elevated on the back of strong earnings growth.

EZCORP's Effective Profit Reinvestment Cycle

While recent performance has been bolstered by precious metals, management is keen on using its recent earnings windfall to fuel CapEx and return cash to shareholders through share buybacks. Full-year FY 2025 EBITDA was ~$181.46M; this operating strength has been reflected in the company's strong balance sheet, which holds $466M in cash & cash equivalents. Management has signaled its interest to reinvest its earnings. In January 2026, EZCORP reported the acquisition of El Buffalo Pawn, which operates 12 pawn stores in Texas, for approximately $27.5M. This acquisition was alongside the majority investment in Founders One.

Management also approved a new share repurchase program for $50M, which followed its previous repurchase program of another $50M. The total impact of repurchases would help reduce the total share count by ~3%, using the current equity value of EZCORP's shares.

EV/EBITDA Multiples Remain Attractive

Despite strong share price performance in 2025, EZCORP's EV/EBITDA levels remain below historical levels during strong financial performance. Ignoring the 2016 multiple expansion when the company was struggling financially, EV/EBITDA is still below 2008 levels.

In EZCORP's peak operating FY, which was 2012, the company generated $222M in EBITDA (FactSet historical data). Given the infancy of this credit cycle and the expectation of worsening credit for consumers, forecasted EBITDA should far exceed historical levels. In FY 2026, analysts (aggregated from FactSet) expect full-year EBITDA of $245M.

Valuation - Expanding EV/EBITDA

Expecting that EZCORP continues to parallel its 2008 stock price rise, I expect investor optimism on EBITDA to grow faster than expected, which would ultimately raise EV/EBITDA multiples. Assuming an 11x EV/EBITDA multiple, which would be below the company's peak multiple during the financial crisis, shares should be trading ~13% higher than current levels. This share price appreciation is in addition to personal optimism on EZCORP's growth in sales and net income as consumer credit options continue to worsen in the United States.

Other Considerations (Risks)

Seasonality of Precious Metals

EZCORP generates profits from jewelry scrap sales, which jumped 129% due to higher precious metals prices. Changes in the price of these precious metals are independent of the company's core operating business segment and may create short-term volatility in EZCORP's sales and profit.

Foreign Exchange Risks

Many of EZCORP's stores are located in Latin America, which exposes the company to foreign currency risk. Short-term changes in these exchange rates can substantially impact the company's bottom line and make it harder to forecast income in future periods.

EZCORP Buy Conclusion

As the credit environment for consumers continues to weaken, dampened by sticky inflation and historically high credit card interest rates, I expect EZCORP to continue to outperform analyst estimates. The company's cyclical strength during the 2008 financial crisis indicates that current share prices have plenty of room to run. Peak operating performance came four years after the 2008 financial crisis. While macro analysis represents forecasting risk, the parallels of the credit environment to 2008 and EZCORP's rising share price are too hard to ignore. Therefore, EZCORP is a buy.
